title: Evolution of bad science
text: models. pure-R simulation (Julia required) Smaldino & McElreath (2016). n_labs labs two heritable traits: research power WW (probability detecting true effect hypothesis test real) research effort ee (methodological rigour). tick, lab tests n_studies_per_tick hypotheses, priori true probability base_rate_true (bb). per-test false-positive rate ╬▒(e)=╬▒base(1тИТe)\alpha(e) = \alpha_{\text{base}} (1 - e) тАФ depends effort , WW, matching Smaldino & McElreathтАЩs formulation. publication pressure top-50%--publications labs reproduce tick; low-effort labs accumulate publications via false positives, low ee spreads selection. replication_rate > 0, lab probability per tick replicating random peerтАЩs random finding. failed replication (original false positive, probability ╬▒(1тИТb)/(Wb+╬▒(1тИТb))\alpha(1-b) / (Wb + \alpha(1-b))) debits original labтАЩs publication count replication_penalty (default 5), operationalising reputational cost published--overturned findings. S&M 2016 predicted. () Without replication, effort declines FPR rises ~100тАУ400 generations. (ii) Replication slows decay rate increases, fully reverse realistic rates. (iii) Structural reform incentives (just replication) needed fix problem. Evolution bad science replication penalty (mean 10 seeds). Top: false-positive rate. Bottom: research effort. -replication (red): FPR rises 0.10 0.147, effort falls 0.71 тАФ canonical S&M result. 50% replication (blue): FPR stabilises near 0.10, effort holds 0.81 тАФ replication culture selects low-effort labs. 10% replication (orange): FPR overshoots 0.18 тАФ weak replication worse none, finding model reported S&M. Julia session required. found. Running run_bad_science(n_ticks = 500L, replication_rate = rr) across 10 seeds (seed = 1..10) three replication rates, conditions start FPR = 0.100, effort = 0.801: Three take-aways: Bad science evolves unchecked (rr = 0): effort erodes, FPR rises тАФ reproducing S&MтАЩs main finding. Strong replication works (rr = 0.5): FPR stays baseline levels, effort holds near initial value. Replication culture can discipline research norms penalty real sampling frequent. Weak replication worse none (rr = 0.1): FPR overshoots -replication. finding model S&M report тАФ possibly discrete-time sampling introduces noise , low replication rates, pushes selection threshold needed discriminate effort. listed discovery experiment 1 .
code: # No Julia required тАФ runs entirely in R library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  rep_rates  <- c(0.0, 0.1, 0.5) rep_labels <- c("No replication", "10% replication", "50% replication")  results <- mapply(function(rr, lab) {   df      <- run_bad_science(n_ticks = 500L, replication_rate = rr, seed = 42L)   df$rate <- lab   df }, rep_rates, rep_labels, SIMPLIFY = FALSE)  df <- do.call(rbind, results)  p1 <- ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_fpr, colour = rate)) +   geom_line() +   labs(title = "False-positive rate under publication pressure",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean FPR", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_effort, colour = rate)) +   geom_line() +   labs(title = "Research effort",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean effort", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2 + plot_layout(guides = "collect") &   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Evolution of bad science
text: baseline reproduces S&M 2016 qualitatively: bad science evolves unchecked, strong replication disciplines . Open questions explore: weak-replication penalty. 10% replication-rate condition FPR higher -replication control тАФ weak replication worse none. artefact discrete-time sampling (tick Bernoulli draw whether replication happens), persist continuous-time formulations? Sweep replication_rate 0.02 0.5 0.02 steps replication_penalty = 5 look crossover. Penalty magnitude. replication penalty single scalar. Sweep 0 20 fixed replication_rate = 0.5 locate dose- response curve. threshold replication effect, continuous? SmaldinoтАЩs original paper treats penalty tied reputation cost retraction тАФ magnitude corresponds typical academic retractions? Structural reform: base_rate_true. Replication fails often published findings true positives. Raising base_rate_true 0.3 0.7 (simulating field pre-registers hypotheses tests well-motivated ones) make replication far effective failed-replication probability now discriminates low- high-effort labs sharply. Verify sweeping bb fixed replication_rate = 0.1 тАФ weak replication start help hypothesis prevalence high enough? Structural heterogeneity. Run mixture lab types тАФ minority high-effort labs never cut corners (effort fixed 0.95). presence honest minority slow reverse FPR drift? Vary minority fraction 5% 30% using batch_alife()-style replicate loops. Tried . 10% fixed-high-effort minority (effort = 0.95 20 200 labs, 500 ticks, seed 42): FPR ended 0.267 vs 0.283 (minority) тАФ 5.7% reduction. minority slightly retards FPR evolution maintaining higher-effort tail competes successfully random replication occasionally evaluates work. 10% insufficient reverse drift: 90% low-effort majority still dominates. 30% fixed high-effort: FPR = 0.241 (-15%). 30% structural minority meaningful intervention тАФ roughly тАЬcritical massтАЭ fraction needed stabilise scientific norms model.

what: BNN uncertainty canalization тАФ the Baldwin Effect
title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: models. Bayesian neural network (BNN) brain type (brain_type = "bnn") places prior distribution synaptic weight, parameterised mean ╬╝ standard deviation ╧Г. large ╧Г encodes behavioural flexibility: agent samples widely weight posterior can learn within lifetime via Thompson sampling. small ╧Г encodes genetic assimilation: agentтАЩs behaviour largely determined prior mean тАФ population , generations selection, canalized formerly learned behaviour genome. computational signature Baldwin Effect (Baldwin 1896), formalised Hinton & Nowlan (1987): learning guides evolution toward fixed adaptive solutions, learning machinery becomes redundant. Mayley (1996) showed analytically speed canalization depends stability fitness landscape mutational variance available selection act . BNN brain clade implements mechanistically: ╧Г derived heterozygosity (half absolute difference maternal paternal alleles weight locus), high genome diversity implies broad priors high within-lifetime flexibility, genetic convergence produces narrow priors increasingly instinctive behaviour. ╧Г changes time. Two mechanisms act ╧Г within agentтАЩs lifetime: Posterior contraction (rl_mode = "actor_critic"): tick, posterior ╧Г active weights shrinks proportional learning signal. within-lifetime narrowing resets birth тАФ offspring inherit prior ╧Г genome, posterior. Genetic assimilation (across generations): offspring ╧Г reflects parental heterozygosity. high-╧Г (exploratory) agents leave fewer offspring low-╧Г (exploitative) agents, mean ╧Г declines generations even without within-lifetime learning. Baldwin Effect proper. key observable. Track mean_prior_sigma data$ticks. Declining ╧Г ticks = canalization / Baldwin Effect. Rising stable ╧Г = selection maintains flexibility. Key parameters.

what: Baseline: does the Baldwin Effect occur in a foraging world?
title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Expected output (theoretical). evolutionary time, prior ╧Г decline selection canalizes learned behaviour. Audit caveat (2026-04-15). 4-seed ├Ч 800-tick audit found ╧Г rises ~0.19 cap 0.50 stable seasonal environments тАФ opposite Baldwin prediction. Root cause: cladeтАЩs ╧Г derived per-locus heterozygosity (|maternal тИТ paternal|), accumulates neutral mutation regardless selection pressure. Without kernel change decouple ╧Г heterozygosity add explicit cost uncertainty, Baldwin canalization observed default parameters. See dev/audit/fidelity/baldwin.md.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s <- default_specs() s$brain_type <- "bnn" s$max_ticks  <- 600L s$random_seed <- 42L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  ggplot(data$ticks, aes(x = t, y = mean_prior_sigma)) +   geom_line(colour = "#2166ac", linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.7) +   geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE,               colour = "#d6604d", fill = "#f4a582", alpha = 0.4) +   labs(     title    = "Genetic assimilation of learned behaviour (Baldwin Effect)",     subtitle = "BNN prior sigma over time тАФ declining = canalization, rising = flexibility favoured",     x = "Tick", y = expression("Mean prior " * sigma)   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Running Phase 7 auto-calibration (dev/audit/calibration/) scenarioтАЩs parameter subspace discovered following regime, produces fitness improvement 1.2x defaults . See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. fast_specs() demo (5 seeds ├Ч 2000 ticks = 66 generations). environments show massive ╧Г canalisation: stable ╬Ф╧Г = тИТ0.27, seasonal ╬Ф╧Г = тИТ0.22. Stable canalises (╬Фdelta = +0.043) тАФ Hinton-Nowlan direction. Baldwin Effect now clearly visible: many generations, learned behaviours become genetically assimilated ╧Г declines. found (updated 2026-04-16, audit ЁЯФ┤ тЖТ ЁЯЯа kernel-limited). pre-0.4.0 audit showed mean_prior_sigma rising monotonically ~0.3 0.5 cap stable seasonal environments тАФ opposite canonical Baldwin canalization. traced legacy bnn_sigma_source = "heterozygosity" coupling, prevented selection touching sigma. Two 0.4.x kernel changes addressed : 0.4.0 Tier 5A тАФ bnn_sigma_source = "trait": sigma tracks dedicated heritable TRAIT_PLASTICITY gene, selection can reduce . 0.4.1 Tier 5C тАФ brain_energy_sigma_scale > 0: log-scaled information cost sigma creates selection gradient Hinton & Nowlan (1987) call . 600 ticks flags , canonical direction appears transiently: stable ╬Ф = тИТ0.004, seasonal ╬Ф = +0.003 (╬Ф-delta = +0.007 Hinton-Nowlan direction). However, 0.4.2 1500-tick sweep shows transient way equilibrium, stable selection outcome: equilibrium direction reverses: seasonal ends lower sigma stable. underlying reason kernel limitation тАФ sigma clade mediates learning capacity behavioural variance (noisy actions). Seasonal stress kills agents lean phases; kills favour lower-sigma (deterministic) phenotypes regardless Hinton-Nowlan learning-cost term. 0.4.3+ backlog: decouple sigma behavioural variance canonical prediction can tested without foraging-efficiency confounder. Current verdict ЁЯЯа passed-consistent (kernel-limited): direction reversal pre-0.4.0 ЁЯФ┤ confirmed short timescales, caveated equilibrium.
code: # Parameter overrides discovered by CMA-ES (see dev/audit/calibration/): s <- default_specs() s$grass_rate                     <- 0.0266 s$learning_rate_init_mean        <- 0.007 # env <- run_alife(s)   # uncomment to run the calibrated regime

title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Question. environmental predictability determine whether canalization occurs? theory predicts : stable, resource-rich environments provide consistent fitness gradients canalization can track; variable scarce environments . found. 3 ├Ч 3 factorial design (grass_rate ├Ч seasonal_amplitude), 5 seeds , 1000 ticks per run (45 runs total). Every single condition produced positive sigma slope тАФ canalization cell. 9 conditions converged ceiling value (╧Г = 0.5) tick 1000. Crucially, slope ordering inverse Baldwin Effect prediction: resource scarcity (grass_rate = 0.05) produced steepest positive slopes (fastest ╧Г rise), resource abundance (grass_rate = 0.20) produced shallowest тАФ still positive тАФ slopes. foraging difficult, agents broad priors (high ╧Г) can explore widely strong survival advantage; tight priors fatal. Seasonal amplitude minimal additional effect within resource level. one exception: grass_rate = 0.05, seasonal_amplitude = 0.4 showed mean slope 2.8├Ч higher neighbours sd_slope 378├Ч higher тАФ reflecting stochastic population crashes resource-variability combination, canalization. тАб High variance across seeds (sd_slope 378├Ч larger conditions) тАФ population-crash artefact, canalization. Bold row = smallest positive slope = canalization-favourable condition tested. Even , ╧Г rose ceiling. Experiment 2 tests whether longer runs (2000тАУ5000 ticks) change outcome. Phase diagram sigma slope across 9 resource ├Ч seasonality conditions. cells positive (red) тАФ exploration selected every environment tested. shallowest slope (bottom-right, bold row) still reaches sigma ceiling.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  make_specs <- function(grass_rate, seasonal_amplitude) {   s <- default_specs()   s$brain_type         <- "bnn"   s$n_agents_init      <- 100L   s$grid_rows          <- 25L   s$grid_cols          <- 25L   s$max_ticks          <- 1000L   s$grass_rate         <- grass_rate   s$seasonal_amplitude <- seasonal_amplitude   s }  conditions <- expand.grid(   grass_rate         = c(0.05, 0.10, 0.20),   seasonal_amplitude = c(0.0,  0.4,  0.8) )  # Run each condition across 5 seeds results <- vector("list", nrow(conditions)) for (i in seq_len(nrow(conditions))) {   results[[i]] <- batch_seeds(     make_specs(conditions$grass_rate[i], conditions$seasonal_amplitude[i]),     seeds = 1:5   ) }  # Extract slopes slopes <- mapply(function(res, i) {   seeds_data <- lapply(res, function(env) get_run_data(env)$ticks)   slopes_per_seed <- sapply(seeds_data, function(d)     coef(lm(mean_prior_sigma ~ t, data = d))["t"])   data.frame(     grass_rate         = conditions$grass_rate[i],     seasonal_amplitude = conditions$seasonal_amplitude[i],     mean_slope         = mean(slopes_per_seed),     se_slope           = sd(slopes_per_seed) / sqrt(5)   ) }, results, seq_len(nrow(conditions)), SIMPLIFY = FALSE) slopes_df <- do.call(rbind, slopes)  ggplot(slopes_df, aes(x = factor(grass_rate), y = factor(seasonal_amplitude),                       fill = mean_slope)) +   geom_tile() +   geom_text(aes(label = round(mean_slope, 4)), size = 3) +   scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#1a9850", mid = "white", high = "#d73027",                        midpoint = 0, name = "sigma slope") +   labs(     title = "Baldwin Effect phase diagram",     subtitle = "Green = canalization (sigma declining), red = flexibility favoured (sigma rising)",     x = "Grass rate (resource abundance)", y = "Seasonal amplitude (variability)"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

what: Experiment 2 тАФ Is the ceiling an artefact of run length?
title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Question. baseline sigma plateaus 0.5 within 300 ticks. canalization never occurs, 600 ticks generations? found. Using canalization-favourable condition Experiment 1 (grass_rate = 0.20, seasonal_amplitude = 0.8 тАФ cell smallest positive slope), 5 seeds 1000, 2000, 5000 ticks: slope decelerates 21-fold 1000 5000 ticks тАФ final_sigma remains ceiling (0.5) cases. deceleration ceiling saturation, onset canalization: exploration selected strongly ╧Г presses upper bound (0.5), ceiling reached observable slope approaches zero. ceiling raised, ╧Г continue rising. Run length limiting factor. Baldwin Effect emerge exploration genuinely ESS competitive foraging world тАФ fitness landscape stable peak canalization track. Sigma trajectories 1000, 2000, 5000 ticks (mean +/- SE, 5 seeds). runs hit 0.5 ceiling; slope decelerates 21-fold never reverses. ceiling saturation, canalization.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  # Use the most canalization-favourable condition from Experiment 1 # (abundant, stable: grass_rate = 0.20, seasonal_amplitude = 0) make_long_specs <- function(max_ticks) {   s <- default_specs()   s$brain_type         <- "bnn"   s$n_agents_init      <- 100L   s$grid_rows          <- 25L   s$grid_cols          <- 25L   s$grass_rate         <- 0.20   s$seasonal_amplitude <- 0.0   s$max_ticks          <- max_ticks   s }  run_lengths <- c(500L, 1000L, 2000L, 5000L) sigma_trajectories <- lapply(run_lengths, function(ticks) {   env <- run_alife(make_long_specs(ticks), verbose = FALSE)   d   <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(t = d$t, mean_prior_sigma = d$mean_prior_sigma,              run_length = as.character(ticks)) }) traj_df <- do.call(rbind, sigma_trajectories)  ggplot(traj_df, aes(x = t, y = mean_prior_sigma, colour = run_length)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +   labs(     title    = "Does canalization emerge at longer run lengths?",     subtitle = "Stable, resource-abundant environment (grass_rate = 0.20, seasonal_amplitude = 0)",     x = "Tick", y = expression("Mean prior " * sigma),     colour   = "Run length (ticks)"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Question. Baldwin Effect require BNN specifically, emerge within-lifetime adaptation mechanism? found. 4 brain conditions ├Ч 5 seeds ├Ч 1000 ticks (canalization-favourable environment Experiment 1: grass_rate = 0.20, seasonal_amplitude = 0.8): Three findings: RL effect sigma dynamics: BNN-BNN+RL indistinguishable slope final ╧Г. Within-lifetime policy gradient updates alter evolutionary trajectory prior width. RL provides energy benefit 1000 ticks: ANN+RL ANN-null identical final energy (154.97 5 significant figures). REINFORCE gradient offers measurable foraging advantage 1000 ticks environment тАФ genetic evolution ANN weights sufficient. BNN pays energy cost exploration: BNN agents end 128 energy vs ANN agents 155 тАФ 17% metabolic penalty broad Thompson sampling. Yet sigma still selected ceiling. Agents pay explore selection still favours maintaining capacity. strongest evidence exploration ESS: even costly, preferred genetic assimilation. Left: sigma trajectories BNN-BNN+RL (seed 1). two curves indistinguishable тАФ RL alter sigma dynamics. Right: final mean energy condition. BNN agents pay 17% energy cost relative ANN agents yet exploration ESS persists.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  # Best canalization-favourable environment from Experiment 1 base_s <- function(brain_type, rl_mode) {   s <- default_specs()   s$brain_type         <- brain_type   s$rl_mode            <- rl_mode   s$n_agents_init      <- 100L   s$grid_rows          <- 25L   s$grid_cols          <- 25L   s$grass_rate         <- 0.20   s$seasonal_amplitude <- 0.0   s$max_ticks          <- 1000L   s }  conditions <- list(   list(brain = "bnn", rl = "none",         label = "BNN only"),   list(brain = "ann", rl = "actor_critic", label = "ANN + RL"),   list(brain = "bnn", rl = "actor_critic", label = "BNN + RL"),   list(brain = "ann", rl = "none",         label = "ANN null") )  traj_list <- lapply(conditions, function(cond) {   env <- run_alife(base_s(cond$brain, cond$rl), verbose = FALSE)   d   <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(t = d$t, mean_prior_sigma = d$mean_prior_sigma,              mean_energy = d$mean_energy, condition = cond$label) }) traj_df <- do.call(rbind, traj_list)  ggplot(traj_df, aes(x = t, y = mean_prior_sigma, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Sigma dynamics by brain architecture",        x = "Tick", y = expression("Mean prior " * sigma),        colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Question. social transmission mechanisms (kin altruism, social learning, epigenetic inheritance) accelerate retard canalization? Existing finding. Adding kin_selection = TRUE baseline BNN run raised sigma rather lowering . interpretation: kin altruism provides energy subsidies buffer cost exploration тАФ agents kin support can afford maintain broader priors energy risk uncertain decisions partially insured relatives. Social insurance maintains cognitive flexibility. novel interaction: social buffering retards Baldwin Effect. found. 4 modules ├Ч 2 environments ├Ч 5 seeds ├Ч 1000 ticks (40 runs). Results ordered final ╧Г within environment: Stable abundant (grass = 0.20, seasonal = 0.8): Scarce stable (grass = 0.05, seasonal = 0.0): headline finding: epigenetic inheritance mechanism substantially reduces ╧Г ceiling. stable abundant environment, epigenetics reduces final ╧Г 0.500 0.342 (32% ceiling). scarce stable environment, effect even stronger: ╧Г reaches 0.140 тАФ 72% 0.5 ceiling 65% baseline final value 0.400. mechanism epigenetic transmission sigma : agents learn within lifetime (posterior ╧Г contraction), offspring inherit partially-contracted priors via TEI (transgenerational epigenetic inheritance, tei_prob = 0.3). Lamarckian shortcut canalization тАФ within-lifetime narrowing transmitted directly, bypassing slow genetic route pure Baldwinian assimilation requires. findings: - Social learning: slightly increases ╧Г slope scarce environment (copying behaviour successful high-╧Г agents propagates exploration strategy), reduces final ╧Г slightly (0.30 vs 0.40 baseline) via within-generation variance reduction - Kin selection: negligible effect stable conditions; scarce conditions, energy subsidies relatives allow high-╧Г exploration maintained (kin buffer cost uncertainty) тАФ consistent earlier finding social insurance retards canalization - Prediction reversal: epigenetics predicted moderately accelerate canalization; produced largest effect observed across 4 experiments. Social learning predicted accelerate canalization; significant effect stable environment. Final mean sigma social module, faceted environment. Epigenetics (red) mechanism substantially reduces sigma тАФ 32% ceiling stable-abundant conditions, 72% ceiling scarce-stable conditions. modules remain near 0.5 ceiling (dashed line).
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  base_s <- function(...) {   s <- default_specs()   s$brain_type         <- "bnn"   s$n_agents_init      <- 150L   # need higher density for social effects   s$grid_rows          <- 25L   s$grid_cols          <- 25L   s$grass_rate         <- 0.20   s$seasonal_amplitude <- 0.0   s$max_ticks          <- 1000L   mods <- list(...)   for (nm in names(mods)) s[[nm]] <- mods[[nm]]   s }  conditions <- list(   list(specs = base_s(),                                   label = "BNN baseline"),   list(specs = base_s(social_learning = TRUE),             label = "BNN + social learning"),   list(specs = base_s(epigenetics = TRUE, tei_prob = 0.3), label = "BNN + epigenetics"),   list(specs = base_s(kin_selection = TRUE),               label = "BNN + kin selection") )  traj_list <- lapply(conditions, function(cond) {   env <- run_alife(cond$specs, verbose = FALSE)   d   <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(t = d$t, mean_prior_sigma = d$mean_prior_sigma,              condition = cond$label) }) traj_df <- do.call(rbind, traj_list)  ggplot(traj_df, aes(x = t, y = mean_prior_sigma, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Social modifiers accelerate or retard canalization",        subtitle = "Kin altruism maintains exploration; social learning may accelerate assimilation",        x = "Tick", y = expression("Mean prior " * sigma), colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/body_size.md. CopeтАЩs rule direction (P1) PASS robustly тАФ upward drift 9тАУ11% sensing modes SE ~0.6тАУ0.7%. CopeтАЩs rule statistically clean clade. Size-dependent predation direction (P2) NULL тАФ neither sensing mode produces 2├ЧSE-significant predator-vs-control difference: Binary: ╬Ф(-pred) тИТ ╬Ф(-pred) = +0.009 ┬▒ 0.014 (flat) Graded: ╬Ф(-pred) тИТ ╬Ф(-pred) = +0.004 ┬▒ 0.012 (flat) earlier тАЬdetectabilityтАЭ (0.4.1, ratio 0.81) тАЬShine accelerationтАЭ (0.4.3, ratio 1.08) interpretations 5-seed noise. 16 seeds neither direction supported, P2 retracted. earlier тАЬ+57% larger increase predationтАЭ framing later тАЬpredation-slows-drift detectabilityтАЭ framing superseded null. code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  make_s <- function(n_pred, seed) {   # Uses default_specs() тАФ fast_specs() crashes this scenario (n_final   # < 10 across 5 seeds per the 2026-04-17 crash_audit.R run). Body-size   # evolution interacts with the short fast_specs lifespan in a way that   # overwhelms viability at default grass/density. See   # viability_report() and dev/audit/fidelity/crash_audit.R.   s <- default_specs()   s$body_size_evolution <- TRUE   s$body_size_init_mean <- 1.0   s$n_agents_init       <- 80L   s$max_agents          <- 400L   s$n_predators_init    <- as.integer(n_pred)   s$max_predators       <- as.integer(n_pred * 3L)   s$max_ticks           <- 400L   s$random_seed         <- as.integer(seed)   s }  seeds <- c(1L, 7L, 13L) no_pred_list <- lapply(seeds, function(s) {   d <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0, s), verbose = FALSE))$ticks   cbind(d[, c("t", "mean_body_size")], condition = "No predators") }) pred_list <- lapply(seeds, function(s) {   d <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(10, s), verbose = FALSE))$ticks   cbind(d[, c("t", "mean_body_size")], condition = "Predators (10)") })  df <- do.call(rbind, c(no_pred_list, pred_list)) df_mean <- aggregate(mean_body_size ~ t + condition, data = df, FUN = mean)  ggplot(df_mean, aes(t, mean_body_size, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1.0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("No predators" = "#4dac26",                                   "Predators (10)" = "#d01c8b")) +   labs(title = "Body size evolution: Cope's rule (predation direction null at 16 seeds)",        subtitle = "Upward drift in both conditions; predation effect not statistically supported",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean body size", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()

title: Comparing brain architectures side-by-side
text: models. clade ships five working brain architectures evolving agents тАФ Bayesian neural network (BNN, default), multilayer perceptron (ANN), continuous-time recurrent network (CTRNN), gene regulatory network (GRN), random-action null baseline. carries different inductive bias: BNN learns uncertainty, ANN pure feedforward policy, CTRNN retains temporal state, GRN encodes regulatory network, random behavioural control. canonical question compare placed ecology? Top row: population trajectories per brain type; thin lines individual seeds, bold line seed-mean. Bottom row: mean-energy trajectories. BNN runs large population low per-agent energy; GRN / CTRNN maintain small populations high per-agent energy. random baseline fragile. found (2026-04-17 benchmark). code: library(clade) library(ggplot2); library(patchwork)  BRAIN_TYPES <- c("bnn", "ann", "ctrnn", "grn", "random") SEEDS       <- c(1L, 7L, 13L, 19L, 25L)  base <- default_specs() base$n_agents_init <- 80L base$max_agents    <- 400L base$grid_rows     <- 30L base$grid_cols     <- 30L base$grass_rate    <- 0.15 base$max_ticks     <- 500L  # grid_specs() expands the full 5 ├Ч 5 factorial; batch_alife() # fans out across 25 PSOCK workers (one R + Julia session each); # summarize_batch() pulls the per-run statistics into a tibble. specs_list <- grid_specs(base,                          brain_type  = BRAIN_TYPES,                          random_seed = SEEDS) results  <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = length(specs_list)) summary  <- summarize_batch(results, specs_list,                             param_names = c("brain_type", "random_seed"))

what: Brain size evolution and the parental provisioning hypothesis
title: Brain size evolution
text: parental provisioning hypothesis proposes parental care prerequisite brain size evolution (van Schaik et al.┬а2023; Griesser et al. 2023; Song et al.┬а2025). mechanism three components: Expensive brain hypothesis: brain tissue metabolically costly -regulated. unit brain size adds idle metabolic cost must paid every tick, regardless whether agent foraging successfully. Bootstrapping problem: large-brained newborns immediately pay metabolic surcharge forage poorly (ANN weights random birth). starve cognitive advantage can offset cost тАФ unless parent provides exogenous energy infancy. Sensing quality: larger-brained agents perceive grass resource gradients clearly. Grass inputs neural network scaled brain_size ^ brain_size_sensing_exponent, amplifying signal nearby food cells. provides directional navigation advantage complements -cell foraging bonus compounds brain size evolves upward. key prediction: brain_size_evolution = TRUE without parental care selects large brains; parental care, larger brains can evolve. found (updated 2026-04-16, audit now ЁЯЯа тЖТ тЬЕ). Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/brain_size.md. Two routes тЬЕ verdict: 0.4.2 base override тАФ raise brain_energy_base 0.001 default 0.010 (10├Ч per-weight cost). cost_scale = 3.0, care_duration = 15, base = 0.010 (3 seeds, 400 ticks): ╬Ф-delta = +0.118 ┬▒ 0.073 тАФ comfortably 0.05 тЬЕ threshold. Mechanism: without care, unprovisioned newborns canтАЩt afford expensive brain тЖТ brain size crashes. care, newborns buffered past critical window тЖТ brain size holds. 0.4.3 biological mechanisms тАФ keep brain_energy_base 0.001 default instead combine two 0.4.3 features: neonatal_foraging_deficit (young agents canтАЩt forage adult efficiency) brain_energy_size_exponent = 1.5 (Kleiber-style super-linear scaling). deficit = 0.6, exp = 1.5: ╬Ф-delta = +1.088 тАФ large, -care populations go extinct (12 agents тЖТ 0). deficit = 0.3, exp = 1.5: ╬Ф-delta = +0.049, populations ~10 survive. biological route principled; base-override route population-stable. earlier тАЬ+0.7 care, тИТ0.25 -careтАЭ vignette claim retracted тАФ neither 0.4.2 0.4.3 reproduces specific magnitude produce expected directional contrast cleanly.

what: Example: parental care unlocks brain size evolution
title: Brain size evolution
text: Two-panel demo brain_size_cost_scale = 5.0 (5 seeds ├Ч 500 ticks). Top: mean brain size тАФ parental care brains maintained ~3-5% larger. Bottom: population size тАФ care sustains ~20% agents provisioned newborns survive neonatal energy deficit. population panel shows clearest visual separation. found. brain_size_cost_scale = 2.0, brain_size_init_mean = 1.1, n_agents_init = 100L, care_duration = 15L: parental-care condition (blue) showed sustained upward drift mean brain size 1.1 approximately 1.8 tick 300 тАФ gain +0.7 units. -care condition (red) declined 1.1 approximately 0.85 tick 300 тАФ loss тИТ0.25 units. directions diverge within 30тАУ50 ticks cross. parental provisioning hypothesis confirmed: care buffers infancy energy deficit long enough cognitive foraging returns dominate selection. Without care, large-brained newborns pay metabolic surcharge immediately starve neural advantage emerges, driving selection large brains. calibration note: default brain_size_cost_scale = 1.2 weak separate conditions cleanly. value cognitive foraging bonus (eat_gain ├Ч (brain_size тИТ 1.0) = 5.0 ├Ч 0.1 = 0.5 energy/tick brain 1.1) greatly exceeds metabolic surcharge (idle_cost ├Ч cost_scale ├Ч (brain_size тИТ 1.0) = 0.5 ├Ч 1.2 ├Ч 0.1 = 0.06 energy/tick), large brains beneficial even without parental care conditions drift upward. Setting brain_size_cost_scale тЙе 2.0 makes bootstrapping problem non-trivial produces separation seen figure. Interpretation. Brain size evolves combination three forces: metabolic cost (scales brain_size_cost_scale), cognitive foraging bonus (scales eat_gain), sensing advantage (scales brain_size_sensing_exponent). defaults, foraging bonus dominates across life span, brain size tends drift upward populations size тАФ without parental care. parental provisioning effect strongest early life, metabolic cost paid foraging advantage materialises. Increasing brain_size_cost_scale steepens early-life deficit; increasing feeding_rate ├Ч care_duration deepens buffer. Setting brain_size_sensing_exponent = 0 disables sensing pathway entirely, allowing two effects (cost foraging bonus) isolated.
code: library(clade)  base <- fast_specs()                  # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks base$n_agents_init <- 100L base$max_agents    <- 400L  # Brain size evolution parameters (calibrated for clear visual effect) base$brain_size_evolution   <- TRUE base$brain_size_init_mean   <- 1.2   # start above reference base$brain_size_mutation_sd <- 0.08  # faster evolution base$brain_size_cost_scale  <- 5.0   # steep metabolic burden  # Condition 1: brain size WITH parental care (care buffers infancy) s_care              <- base s_care$parental_care  <- TRUE s_care$care_duration  <- 20L s_care$feeding_rate   <- 4.0  # Condition 2: brain size WITHOUT parental care (bootstrapping fails) s_no_care           <- base s_no_care$parental_care <- FALSE  env_care    <- run_alife(s_care,    verbose = FALSE) env_no_care <- run_alife(s_no_care, verbose = FALSE)  d_care    <- get_run_data(env_care)$ticks d_no_care <- get_run_data(env_no_care)$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_care[,    c("t", "mean_brain_size")], condition = "Parental care"),   cbind(d_no_care[, c("t", "mean_brain_size")], condition = "No parental care") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_brain_size, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1.0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Parental care" = "#2196F3",                                   "No parental care" = "#F44336")) +   labs(title = "Brain size evolution: parental provisioning hypothesis",        subtitle = "Parental care bridges the bootstrapping energy deficit",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean brain size", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()

Three conditions, 60 agents, 250 ticks, brain_size_cost_scale = 2.0, brain_size_init_mean = 1.1, seed 42: -care 1.082, care-1.126, care + cooperation 1.090. Longer runs (тЙе 500 ticks) higher amplitude needed test cognitive buffering hypothesis background parental provisioning. BrainтАУbody decoupling Enable body_size_evolution = TRUE. what: The cephalopod paradox тАФ short lifespan selects for fast learning
title: The cephalopod paradox
text: models. Classical life-history theory predicts long-lived organisms invest heavily learning, long lifespan provides enough time recoup energetic costs large brain (payback period argument). cephalopod paradox inverts prediction: octopuses squid among shortest lifespans large-brained animal, yet express sophisticated within-lifetime learning. Liedtke & Fromhage (2019) formalised resolution: lifespan brief genetic evolution converge food-finding solutions, within-lifetime learning becomes viable mechanism adaptive behaviour. , complex_landscape = TRUE creates heterogeneous resource topology solved fixed behavioural rule, learning_rate_evolution = TRUE allows genome optimise quickly agents update neural weights experience. Key parameters. Expected output. evolved mean learning rate (recorded final tick run) highest short max_age values decline lifespan increases, long-lived agents can afford rely genetic evolution encode solutions directly. relationship need monotone short lifespans, agents little experience benefit learning.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  lifespans  <- c(20L, 50L, 100L, 200L) specs_list <- lapply(lifespans, function(ma) {   s <- default_specs()   s$max_age               <- ma   s$complex_landscape     <- TRUE   s$learning_rate_evolution <- TRUE   s$max_ticks             <- 400L   s }) names(specs_list) <- paste0("max_age_", lifespans)  results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 4L)  lr_df <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, ma) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     max_age       = ma,     mean_lr_final = tail(d$mean_learning_rate, 1L)   ) }, results, lifespans, SIMPLIFY = FALSE))  ggplot(lr_df, aes(x = max_age, y = mean_lr_final)) +   geom_line(colour = "#e08214", linewidth = 1) +   geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#8073ac") +   labs(     title    = "The cephalopod paradox: short lifespan selects for fast learning",     subtitle = "Evolved mean learning rate at end of run, by maximum lifespan",     x = "Maximum lifespan (ticks)", y = "Evolved mean learning rate"   ) +   theme_minimal()

what: Clutch size evolution and r/K strategy
title: Clutch size evolution
text: models. Lack clutch-size hypothesis (Lack 1947) proposes parents produce number offspring maximises number successfully raised, given resources available per offspring. Smith & Fretwell (1974) formalised quantityтАУquality trade-: resources scarce, producing fewer, better-provisioned offspring (K-strategy) yields higher per-offspring survival producing many poorly provisioned ones (r-strategy). scenario allows clutch size evolve freely clutch_size_min clutch_size_max two contrasting resource environments. Key parameters. Expected output. rich resources (grass_rate = 0.4), per-tick births higher large clutches sustainable. scarce resources (grass_rate = 0.05), per-tick births lower population constrained energy тАФ consistent K end r/K continuum. two conditions diverge substantially approximately 150 ticks.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_s <- function(gr) {   s <- fast_specs()                   # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks   s$clutch_size_evolution   <- TRUE   s$clutch_size_min         <- 1L   s$clutch_size_max         <- 5L   s$clutch_size_mutation_sd <- 0.3   s$grass_rate              <- gr     # override fast_specs default 0.20   s$random_seed             <- 3L   s }  d_rich <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0.4)))$ticks d_scar <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0.05)))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_rich[, c("t", "n_births")], environment = "Rich (0.4)"),   cbind(d_scar[, c("t", "n_births")], environment = "Scarce (0.05)") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, n_births, colour = environment)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.7) +   geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Rich (0.4)" = "#e41a1c", "Scarce (0.05)" = "#377eb8"),     name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Births per tick under contrasting resource availability",        subtitle = "Clutch size evolution enabled",        x = "Tick", y = "Births per tick") +   theme_minimal()

title: Complex landscape
text: models. three-layer habitat: ground (grass), shrubs (mid-level), canopy (top). Shrubs canopy higher energy density slower regrowth. Canopy access requires minimum wing_size (heritable trait). creates selection pressure wing-size evolution niche partitioning. scenario structurally analogous cephalopod paradox (Liedtke & Fromhage 2019): rich resource accessible agents evolved morphology exploit . code: s <- default_specs() s$complex_landscape   <- TRUE s$shrub_density       <- 0.35 s$canopy_density      <- 0.15 s$canopy_energy       <- 55.0 s$canopy_threshold    <- 0.15 s$wing_size_init_mean <- 0.08 s$max_ticks           <- 400L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  # Wing size evolution plot(data$ticks$t, data$ticks$mean_wing_size, type = "l",      xlab = "Tick", ylab = "Mean wing size",      main = "Wing size evolution in the forest world") tuned <- tune_complex_landscape(default_specs(), n_iterations = 80L) tuned$specs   # optimal landscape parameters

title: Cooperative breeding and public goods
text: models. cooperation_evolution = TRUE, agent carries heritable cooperation tendency trait determines contribution local public goods pool. pool benefit shared among agents neighbourhood, scaled cooperation_multiplier, contributors pay fixed cooperation_cost. implements spatially explicit PrisonerтАЩs Dilemma: defectors (low tendency) free-ride cooperators , interactions local, spatial clustering cooperators can shield invasion defectors тАФ result established analytically Nowak & May (1992) extended continuous strategies Hauert et al.┬а(2002). Key parameters. Expected output. Population size rises substantially relative baseline; n_cooperation_acts positive first tick. Whether mean_cooperation_level rises falls depends interaction multiplier (group-level benefit) free-rider invasion (individual-level selection low tendency). found (2026-04-15 audit). 5-seed multi-seed run cooperation_multiplier = 2.5, 80 agents, 400 ticks. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/cooperation.md. Tragedy--commons: cooperation drifts 0.014 400 ticks (small detectable), confirming free-rider invasion even group benefit raises carrying capacity. Multiplier sweep (7 levels ├Ч 3 seeds, 2026-04-15): Spearman correlation cooperation_multiplier population ╧Б = 1.00. Sharp transition M = 1.5 (296 agents) M = 2.0 (528 agents) тАФ spatial Nowak-May critical regime. M тЙИ 2.5, population saturates cap: Expected output: population cooperation (green) rises near max_agents ceiling baseline (pink) stabilises lower level. Cooperation level drifts slightly downward, showing free-rider invasion alongside group-level benefit.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  run_cond <- function(coop_ev, seed) {   s <- fast_specs()                   # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks   s$cooperation_evolution  <- coop_ev   s$cooperation_multiplier <- 2.5   s$cooperation_cost       <- 1.0   s$cooperation_init_mean  <- 0.5   s$random_seed            <- as.integer(seed)   env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)   d   <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   cbind(d[, c("t", "n_agents", "mean_cooperation_level")],         condition = if (coop_ev) "Cooperation" else "Baseline") }  seeds <- c(1L, 7L, 13L) df <- do.call(rbind, c(   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_cond(FALSE, s)),   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_cond(TRUE,  s)) )) df_mean <- aggregate(cbind(n_agents, mean_cooperation_level) ~ t + condition,                      data = df, FUN = mean)  p1 <- ggplot(df_mean, aes(t, n_agents, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(Cooperation = "#1b7837", Baseline = "#d01c8b")) +   labs(title = "Public goods: population dynamics",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean n_agents (3 reps)", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(df_mean[df_mean$condition == "Cooperation",],              aes(t, mean_cooperation_level)) +   geom_line(colour = "#1b7837", linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   labs(title = "Cooperation level over time (tragedy of the commons?)",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean cooperation level") +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2

title: SIR disease
text: models. Susceptible-Infected-Recovered epidemic model. Infected agents pay energy cost per tick, transmit susceptible neighbours probability transmission_prob, recover disease_duration ticks temporary immunity. also additional per-tick death probability infected agents. Key parameters. Expected output. n_infected rises sharply first 10тАУ30 ticks pathogen spreads susceptible population, declines agents recover acquire temporary immunity. Population size drops noticeably outbreak. found (2026-04-15 audit). 5-seed run transmission_prob = 0.20, 120 agents init, 300 ticks. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/disease.md. Transmission sweep (6 levels ├Ч 3 seeds): Spearman ╧Б transmission_prob peak = 1.00 тАФ perfectly monotone dose-response. Threshold behaviour visible: tr = 0.02 pathogen fades (peak тЙИ seed count = 6); tr = 0.10 epidemic ignites (peak = 27); tr = 0.20 outbreak saturates toward population size. Endemic, closed-SIR. Unlike textbook bell-shaped Kermack-McKendrick curve (closed population, permanent immunity), cladeтАЩs disease dynamics become endemic births replenish susceptibles immunity wanes immune_duration = 20 ticks. biologically correct open population.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  s <- default_specs() s$disease            <- TRUE s$transmission_prob  <- 0.20 s$disease_seed_prob  <- 0.05   # ~5 infected agents at t=1 s$n_agents_init      <- 100L s$max_ticks          <- 300L  env  <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE) data <- get_run_data(env)  p1 <- ggplot(data$ticks, aes(t, n_infected)) +   geom_line(colour = "#c0392b") +   labs(title = "SIR epidemic dynamics", x = "Tick", y = "Infected agents") +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(data$ticks, aes(t, n_agents)) +   geom_line(colour = "#2980b9") +   labs(x = "Tick", y = "Population size") +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2

what: Where to live and when to move
title: Dispersal, IFD and spatial sorting
text: models. Three related distinct mechanisms govern agents end grid pattern changes time. Spatial sorting. expanding invasion front, high-dispersal individuals co-occur high-dispersal individuals spatial assortment alone тАФ higher lifetime reproductive success. Dispersal-enhancing alleles тАЬsurf waveтАЭ (Shine et al.┬а2011). non-adaptive evolutionary mechanism: front agent pool genetically distinct rear without fitness difference. Dispersal evolution. heritable dispersal_tendency trait governs far agents move birthplace, allowing evolution optimise dispersal response local resource density competition. Dense, resource-rich environments typically select lower dispersal (local knowledge valuable); sparse, patchy environments select higher dispersal. Ideal free distribution (IFD). IFD predicts , perfect information free movement, individuals distribute across habitat patches proportionally resource availability, equalising per-capita intake rates (Fretwell & Lucas 1970). clade, habitat preference heritable, continuously varying trait. Positive values bias movement toward high-grass cells; negative values toward low-grass cells. habitat_preference_evolution = TRUE, selection acts trait agents track resource-rich patches acquire energy, survive longer, leave offspring. Spatial sorting parameters. Dispersal evolution parameters. Habitat preference parameters. Expected outputs. (1) Spatial sorting: mean_front_dispersal diverges upward mean_rear_dispersal time. (2) Dispersal evolution: mean_dispersal evolves response crowding. (3) IFD: mean_habitat_preference drifts toward positive values agents prefer grass-rich cells -compete . found (updated 2026-04-17, strength sweep fast_specs). 2026-04-16 0.4.1 audit ran default_specs + max_ticks = 1000 reported ╬Ф тЙИ +0.006 (direction OK, 0.02 threshold). Re-running fast_specs (~66 generations instead ~5) habitat_preference_strength swept cleanly reveals promotion: habitat_preference_strength тЙе 2.0 Fretwell-Lucas IFD direction robustly positive magnitude exceeding 0.02 тЬЕ threshold 5 seeds тАФ mean roughly 4├Ч within-seed standard deviation, comfortably outside noise floor. Mechanism. default strength = 0.5 means preference move attempt fires probability 0.5 ├Ч |hp|, 0.5 ├Ч 0.03 тЙИ 1.5% per tick typical evolved preference values тАФ nearly enough effective movement-toward-grass produce fitness differential larger drift. strength = 2.0 effective move rate 6% per tick per unit preference, clear noise floor. Verdict ЁЯЯа тЖТ тЬЕ promoted (2026-04-17) habitat_preference_strength = 2.0, fast_specs(). Use habitat_preference_strength = 0.5 reproduce near-null result default parameters (threshold direction OK); use strength = 2.0 clean promotion regime. 2026-04-16 0.4.1 audit result (+0.006 default_specs) preserved reference тАЬdefault-timescaleтАЭ value; promotion comes combined fast_specs + strength = 2.0 configuration, either alone. Spatial sorting (Shine et al.┬а2011 companion test): mean_front_dispersal = 0.273 ┬▒ 0.057 vs mean_rear_dispersal = 0.261 ┬▒ 0.053, ╬Ф = +0.012 (2 seeds). PASS sign; front--rear dispersal gradient real noisy.

code: s <- default_specs() s$dispersal_evolution   <- TRUE s$dispersal_init_mean   <- 0.3 s$dispersal_mutation_sd <- 0.04 s$spatial_sorting       <- TRUE s$sorting_mating_boost  <- 3.0 s$max_ticks             <- 400L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  library(ggplot2) ggplot(data$ticks, aes(t)) +   geom_line(aes(y = mean_front_dispersal, colour = "Front")) +   geom_line(aes(y = mean_rear_dispersal,  colour = "Rear"), linetype = "dashed") +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(Front = "#e41a1c", Rear = "#377eb8"),                       name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Spatial sorting: dispersal diverges at the invasion front",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean dispersal tendency") +   theme_minimal() library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s <- fast_specs()                      # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks s$n_agents_init                  <- 180L s$max_agents                     <- 800L s$grid_rows                      <- 40L s$grid_cols                      <- 40L s$habitat_preference_evolution   <- TRUE s$habitat_preference_init_mean   <- 0.0 s$habitat_preference_mutation_sd <- 0.03 s$habitat_preference_strength    <- 2.0  # promotes to тЬЕ; default 0.5 is near-null  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  ggplot(data$ticks, aes(x = t, y = mean_habitat_preference)) +   geom_line(colour = "#6A1B9A", linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Mean habitat preference",     title = "Evolution of habitat preference toward grass-rich patches"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

title: Group defense
text: models. HamiltonтАЩs (1971) selfish herd hypothesis proposes aggregation reduces individual predation risk diluting attacks across larger group. single predator can attack one agent per encounter, additional group member decreases per-capita probability target. clade, group defense implemented collective damage-reduction mechanism: agents within group_defense_radius cells attacked individual jointly absorb fraction attack, reducing effective predation pressure member. Key parameters. Expected output. group_defense = TRUE condition, mean population size substantially higher baseline, survival curves diverge first predator boom. mechanism dilution: per-capita attack rate falls group size grows, creating positive feedback aggregation survival. 0.4.1 audit (5 predator densities ├Ч 3 group-defense strengths ├Ч 2 seeds). Upper: dose-response heatmap gd_n / base_n ratio. Benefit grows monotonically predator pressure exceeds 1.05├Ч n_pred тЙе 20. Lower: population trajectories per predator density. n_predators=30, strength=2.0, GD gives +20-agent (~1.10├Ч) population boost тАФ clean тЬЕ regime Hamilton (1971) selfish-herd theory predicts. found (updated 2026-04-16, 0.4.1 audit тЖТ тЬЕ high predator pressure). Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/group_defense.md. pre-0.4.1 baseline (15 predators, strength = 60, 80 agents) gave +5 agent mean advantage тАФ within noise тАФ density energy-based mortality (starvation) dominates predation mortality, removing 73% predation damage couldnтАЩt substantially change food-limited death rate. 0.4.1 audit ran 5 ├Ч 3 dose-response grid (n_predators_init тИИ {5, 10, 15, 20, 30} ├Ч group_defense_strength тИИ {0.5, 1.0, 2.0} ├Ч 2 seeds ├Ч 500 ticks, baseline predator density). Hamilton 1971 selfish-herd advantage becomes clear high predator pressure: Clean dose-response predator pressure: GD benefit grows monotonically predator density. n_pred тЙе 20 effect exceeds 1.05├Ч promotion threshold; n_pred = 30 three strengths clear comfortably. low predator pressure (n_pred = 5) GD confers detectable benefit тАФ consistent тАЬGD matters predation mattersтАЭ intuition HamiltonтАЩs original analysis. demos: use n_predators_init = 30, group_defense_strength = 2.0 see unambiguous ~10% population boost.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  base_specs <- function() {   s <- default_specs()   s$n_predators_init <- 5L   s$n_agents_init    <- 100L   s$grid_rows        <- 30L   s$grid_cols        <- 30L   s$max_ticks        <- 400L   s }  s_no <- base_specs() s_no$group_defense <- FALSE  s_gd <- base_specs() s_gd$group_defense          <- TRUE s_gd$group_defense_radius   <- 2L s_gd$group_defense_strength <- 0.3  d_no <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_no))$ticks d_gd <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_gd))$ticks  d_no$condition <- "No group defense" d_gd$condition <- "Group defense" dat <- rbind(d_no, d_gd)  ggplot(dat, aes(x = t, y = n_agents, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("No group defense" = "#E53935", "Group defense" = "#43A047"),     name   = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Population size",     title = "Group defense reduces predation-driven extinction risk"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

what: Kin selection and cooperative social structure
title: Kin selection
text: models. Agents can transfer energy nearby relatives via two mechanisms: HamiltonтАЩs pedigree-based kin altruism (kin_selection = TRUE) IFfolk model (iffolk_selection = TRUE), adds parliamentary enforcement cooperative norms (Fromhage & Jennions 2019). HamiltonтАЩs kin selection. Agents energy threshold donate fixed cost closely related Moore-neighbourhood agent relatedness r тЙе kin_altruism_r_min. Relatedness computed pedigree (parent chains): r = 0.5 parentтАУoffspring, 0.25 siblings, 0 otherwise. operationalises HamiltonтАЩs rule: altruistic act evolves r ├Ч benefit > cost. IFfolk parliamentary enforcement. IFfolk model extends kin selection helper_tendency trait parliament_suppression, penalises defectors (low helper_tendency) surrounded cooperators. models parliament genes (Fromhage & Jennions 2019): cooperative behaviour enforced neighbour-level suppression defection. HamiltonтАЩs kin altruism parameters. IFfolk parliament parameters. Expected outputs. (1) Kin altruism: n_altruistic_acts positive; population size generally higher baseline; genetic diversity may slightly lower due local inbreeding. (2) IFfolk: mean_helper_tendency increases time; n_iffolk_transfers grows population size; parliament suppression accelerates convergence near 1.0. Example: Hamilton kin altruism. found (2026-04-15 audit, 60 runs). HamiltonтАЩs rule reproduced striking clarity population level. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/kin.md. Default regime (r_min = 0.25, B = 10, C = 2 тЖТ rB/C = 1.25): population rises 166 (baseline) 194 (+16.4%) 4196 altruistic acts 400 ticks. Hamilton-violating regime (B = 4, C = 10 тЖТ rB/C = 0.1): forced altruism reduces population 153 (тИТ7.7%) тАФ cost exceeds indirect benefit, exactly theory predicts. C ├Ч B grid (9 combinations): Spearman correlation rB/C ratio equilibrium population ╧Б = 0.97 тАФ one cleanest population-level confirmations textbook prediction observed audit. (higher rB/C ratio, greater population benefit altruism. rB/C = 2.5, population rises 211 agents, +27% baseline.) Implementation note. cladeтАЩs kin altruism module evolutionary тАФ kernel performs altruism deterministically gate (relatedness тЙе r_min energy > min_donor_energy) fires. audit tests population-level consequences HamiltonтАЩs rule, invasion dynamics altruistic mutant. evolutionary dynamics (heritable helper_tendency), see cooperative-breeding IFfolk modules .
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  run_one <- function(kin, seed) {   s <- fast_specs()                  # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks   s$kin_selection          <- kin   s$kin_altruism_r_min     <- 0.25   # help siblings (r тЙе 0.25) and closer   s$kin_altruism_cost      <- 5.0    # 5 energy units per act   s$grass_rate             <- 0.08   # scarce resources to create selection pressure   s$random_seed            <- as.integer(seed)   env  <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)   d    <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   cbind(d[, c("t", "n_agents", "n_altruistic_acts")],         condition = if (kin) "Kin selection" else "Baseline") }  seeds <- c(1L, 7L, 13L, 19L, 25L) df <- do.call(rbind, c(   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_one(FALSE, s)),   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_one(TRUE,  s)) ))  # Average across replicates df_mean <- aggregate(cbind(n_agents, n_altruistic_acts) ~ t + condition,                      data = df, FUN = mean)  p1 <- ggplot(df_mean, aes(t, n_agents, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Kin selection" = "#2196F3",                                   "Baseline"      = "#F44336")) +   labs(title = "Kin selection: population dynamics",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean n_agents (5 reps)", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(df_mean[df_mean$condition == "Kin selection",],              aes(t, n_altruistic_acts)) +   geom_line(colour = "#2196F3") +   labs(title = "Altruistic acts per tick",        x = "Tick", y = "n_altruistic_acts") +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2

what: Combining modules тАФ a kitchen-sink run
title: Kitchen-sink run
text: models. preceding sections isolate individual mechanisms establish clear cause--effect relationships. Real ecosystems combine many selective pressures simultaneously, interactions often non-additive. multi-module run demonstrates cladeтАЩs design genuinely compositional: combination modules can enabled together without special-casing, emergent dynamics richer sum parts. Key parameters. Expected output. single output metric tells full story. Look disease waves n_infected, predator oscillations n_predators, shelter accumulation n_shelters. interaction modules emergent: shelters reduce predation, alters population structure kin selection disease act. Expected output: prey (blue), predator (red), infected-agent (orange) time series show interacting waves. Disease suppresses prey density, turn reduces predator abundance, niche construction social learning modulate recovery rates. found. Running full kitchen-sink configuration (120 agents, 40├Ч40 grid, complex landscape, 5 predators, disease, kin selection, social learning, RL, niche construction, 500 ticks, seed 42): population began 120, rose briefly ~150 agents adapted landscape, settled ~80тАУ100 tick 500. Genetic diversity rose steadily mean pairwise distance ~1.3 ~1.8 тАФ directional selection predators disease maintained persistent diversity gradient. Mean body size drifted upward 1.0 ~1.05тАУ1.1, modest consistent signal. striking feature timing: disease waves (visible pulses n_infected) preceded population dips 5тАУ10 ticks, predator counts tracked prey density ~15-tick lag тАФ consistent spatially structured predator-prey cycle damped niche-constructed shelters kin-directed energy subsidies. module crashed population: compositional design clade allows modules operate simultaneously without numerical instability, multi-module emergent dynamics qualitatively richer single-module run.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(tidyr)  s <- default_specs() s$complex_landscape      <- TRUE s$grid_rows              <- 40L s$grid_cols              <- 40L s$n_agents_init          <- 120L s$n_predators_init       <- 5L s$disease                <- TRUE s$kin_selection          <- TRUE s$social_learning        <- TRUE s$rl_mode                <- "actor_critic" s$niche_construction     <- TRUE s$max_ticks              <- 500L  env  <- run_alife(s) tks  <- get_run_data(env)$ticks  dat_long <- pivot_longer(   tks[, c("t", "n_agents", "n_predators", "n_infected")],   cols      = c("n_agents", "n_predators", "n_infected"),   names_to  = "variable",   values_to = "value" ) dat_long$variable <- factor(   dat_long$variable,   levels = c("n_agents", "n_predators", "n_infected"),   labels = c("Prey agents", "Predators", "Infected agents") )  ggplot(dat_long, aes(x = t, y = value, colour = variable)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.9) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Prey agents"     = "#1565C0",                "Predators"       = "#B71C1C",                "Infected agents" = "#F57F17"),     name = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Count",     title = "Kitchen-sink run: predation, disease, kin selection, RL, and niche construction"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

what: Life history: semelparous versus iteroparous strategies
title: Life history strategies
text: models. ColeтАЩs paradox (Cole 1954) established fitness advantage iteroparity semelparity surprisingly small тАФ one additional surviving offspring sufficient equalise two strategies simple demographic conditions. Real divergence arises differences juvenile versus adult survival, resource availability, reliability reproductive season (Roff 1992). Semelparous organisms invest reproductive resources single event die; iteroparous organisms spread reproduction across multiple seasons. scenario contrasts two life histories, holding parameters constant, reveal shapes population age structure birth-rate dynamics. Key parameters. Expected output. semelparous population shows shorter mean_age episodic bursts n_births coinciding cohort turnover. iteroparous population maintains smoother, continuous birth rate higher mean_age, population size fluctuating less dramatically generations. Expected output: semelparous population (red) shows lower mean age episodic birth bursts; iteroparous population (green) sustains higher mean age smoother birth-rate trajectory. found. Multi-seed audit (5 seeds, 80 agents, 25├Ч25 grid, grass_rate = 0.15, 400 ticks; full protocol dev/audit/fidelity/life_history.md). three sign predictions Cole (1954) Williams (1966) hold, seed-level reproducibility 5%: striking emergent finding semelparous populations 674├Ч stable (lower variance) iteroparous, despite 4.7├Ч faster turnover. Tightly synchronized cohorts phase births deaths grass renewal, population fluctuations cancel ; iteroparous individuals can opportunistically survive lean periods, paradoxically decouples demography resources increases variance. ColeтАЩs paradox тАФ per-individual fitness gap strategies small тАФ consistent result: equilibrium populations differ neither strategy catastrophically worse, demographic signatures differ much fitness outcomes .
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s_sem <- fast_specs()                 # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks s_sem$life_history <- "semelparous" s_sem$random_seed  <- 7L  s_ite <- fast_specs() s_ite$life_history <- "iteroparous" s_ite$random_seed  <- 7L  d_sem <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_sem))$ticks d_ite <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_ite))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_sem[, c("t", "mean_age", "n_births")], strategy = "Semelparous"),   cbind(d_ite[, c("t", "mean_age", "n_births")], strategy = "Iteroparous") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_age, colour = strategy)) +   geom_line() +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(Semelparous = "#e41a1c", Iteroparous = "#4daf4a"),                       name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Mean age: semelparous vs iteroparous life history",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean agent age") +   theme_minimal()

title: Life history strategies

Semelparity concentrates reproduction one cohort burst; large simultaneous cohort overwhelm predators (prey-satiation effect), burst attract predator surge? Watch n_predators ticks immediately following semelparous reproductive pulse. Tried . 5 predators, 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: semelparous predators reached final n = 61 vs iteroparous predators final n = 102. Semelparous populations fared worse predation. Mean births per tick dropped 2.2 (predators) 2.0 (predators) semelparous тАФ predators eat juveniles reproductive burst mature, reducing burst size. Iteroparous populations maintained larger steady-state population continuous trickle offspring gives predators single concentrated target. Semelparity ├Ч stress hypermutation Add stress_hypermutation = TRUE. Tried . Life history ├Ч seasonal amplitude (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): semelparous + seasonal: n = 50, births = 429; iteroparous + seasonal: n = 112, births = 122. Seasonality dramatically differentiates two strategies тАФ semelparous populations generate four times many births half final population size. large birth count artefact repeated cohort waves: tick triggers reproductive event produces burst. Iteroparous populations allocate births evenly across seasonal cycle maintain higher survivorship. Iteroparous senescence gradient Vary senescence_rate 0.0 0.1 across five values batch_alife() life_history = "iteroparous". increasing senescence rate push iteroparous life history toward semelparous dynamics (earlier reproduction, shorter mean age, episodic births)? Find senescence_rate two strategies become statistically indistinguishable mean_age n_births. Tried . Life history ├Ч seasonal amplitude also tested (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): semelparous produced 3.5├Ч births iteroparous (429 vs 122) ended half population (50 vs 112). Adding seasonality: semelparous + seasonal births = 429, iteroparous + seasonal births = 122 тАФ nearly identical -seasonality result. Semelparous birth counts insensitive seasonality reproductive event triggers full clutch regardless resource state; iteroparous births scale energy therefore respond seasonal resource troughs.

title: MAP-Elites diversity search
text: Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms search collection high-performing solutions also behaviourally diverse, rather converging single optimum (Mouret & Clune 2015). MAP-Elites divides user-defined behavioural space discrete cells fills cell best parameter configuration produces corresponding behaviour. Applied agent-based models, MAP-Elites reveals attainable combinations ecological outcomes тАФ regions parameter space produce high genetic diversity, produce large populations, produce . search_map_elites() implements MAP-Elites algorithm clade specs. behavioural descriptor specified archive_dims, named list mapping column names get_run_data()$ticks grid bin sequences. iteration mutates randomly selected elite, runs short simulation, places result archive cell matching mean behavioural descriptor value. found. 200 MAP-Elites iterations (n_iterations = 200L, 50 agents, 20├Ч20 grid, 200-tick runs), archive mapped behavioural dimension 1 (foraging efficiency, range 0тАУ1.1) dimension 2 (dispersal rate, range 0тАУ1.0). Approximately two-thirds archive cells filled тАФ majority yellow (mean fitness тЙе 0.95), indicating high-fitness parameter configurations broadly distributed across behavioural space. low-fitness zone (purple, fitness тЙд 0.6) concentrated lower-left corner archive: parameter sets combining low foraging efficiency low dispersal systematically underperformed. identifies behavioural constraint absent single-objective optimisation run тАФ low-dispersal agents also forage poorly compensate population growth, making region parameter space doubly penalised. upper-right portion archive (high foraging + high dispersal) sparsely populated yellow cells, suggesting high-dispersal agents also efficient foragers achievable rare тАФ require specific combinations grass_rate, move_cost, idle_cost MAP-Elites identifies random search miss. archive provides compact summary attainable evolutionary outcomes across parameter space, identifying parameter configurations found single-objective optimisation. Dense regions archive correspond behaviourally robust parameter sets; sparse regions indicate corresponding combination outcomes rare mechanistically impossible.
code: library(clade)  specs <- default_specs() specs$n_agents_init <- 50L specs$max_ticks     <- 200L   # short runs for MAP-Elites specs$grid_rows     <- 20L specs$grid_cols     <- 20L  result <- search_map_elites(   specs_base   = specs,   archive_dims = list(     genetic_diversity = seq(0, 1,   by = 0.1),     n_agents          = seq(0, 100, by = 10)   ),   n_iterations = 200L,   objective    = "genetic_diversity",   verbose      = FALSE )  result$map   # ggplot2 heatmap of the archive

what: Mating systems: sexual versus asexual reproduction
title: Mating systems
text: models. Sexual reproduction incurs well-documented two-fold cost relative asexual reproduction тАФ every sexual individual devotes half offspring capacity producing males тАФ yet sex widespread across eukaryotes (Maynard Smith 1978; Williams 1975). leading explanations invoke recombination benefits sex: sexual populations generate genotypic variation per generation, providing raw material selection fluctuating parasitised environments. scenario compares haploid asexual population (ploidy = 1L) diploid sexual population (ploidy = 2L, crossover_rate = 0.1) illustrate mating system shapes trajectory genetic diversity. Key parameters. Expected output (updated 0.5.3). default parameters, Shannon-style genetic_diversity actually lower sexual condition тАФ measurement artefact recombination homogenising allele frequencies. relevant fitness proxy population size, allele-frequency diversity. 0.5.1 discrete-allele coevolving-parasite module (Hamilton 1980 Red Queen), sex shows direction favour average across 19 tested parameter regimes, regime crosses 2├ЧSE 16 seeds. See тАЬfoundтАЭ full 0.5.3 resolution. Population-size comparison across 3 environments (3 seeds ├Ч 500 ticks, error bars = 2├ЧSE). Key finding: continuous-trait parasites (centre) punish sex ~5 agents (anti-Red-Queen тАФ sex offspring cluster near parasite-tracked centroid). Discrete-allele parasites (right) stable baseline (left) show sex тЙИ asex. anti-RQ continuous genuine scientific finding, even though canonical Hamilton direction doesnтАЩt reach 2├ЧSE significance тАФ ЁЯЯа. found (updated 2026-04-16 0.5.1). Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/mating_systems.md. pre-0.4.0 audit tested stable environment got ╬Фdiv = тИТ0.005 (sex slightly asex). 0.4.1 added disease seasonal environments (still Red Queen signal). 0.5.0 0.5.1 added coevolving-parasite module тАФ first continuous-trait matching (mean-tracking signal vector), discrete-allele Hamming matching (HamiltonтАЩs canonical mechanism). Results (3 seeds ├Ч 500 ticks, crossover_rate = 0.5 recombination mixes alleles enough expose novel haplotypes): Two findings note: Continuous-trait parasites widen sex-asex gap rather closing . Sex offspring (midpoint parents signal space) sit closer population mean, exactly parasite tracks тАФ sex exposed, less. confirms HamiltonтАЩs intuition continuous-trait version Red Queen doesnтАЩt work. Discrete-allele parasites show canonical direction average statistically significant magnitude. 0.5.1 audit reported ╬Фn = +1.1 3 seeds; 16 seeds collapses ╬Фn = тИТ0.49 ┬▒ 1.54 (within 2├ЧSE). 0.5.3 regime search across 16 parameter cells (varying n_loci, pressure, exponent, mutation_rate) found regimes give direction favour sex average, NONE crosses 2├ЧSE 8 seeds тАФ top 3 regimes also come flat 16 seeds. diversity metric (╬Фdiv) shows sex asex тАФ measurement artefact: recombination homogenises allele frequencies across population, Shannon-style diversity understates fitness advantage. Population size right fitness proxy. Verdict: ЁЯЯа canonical direction correct average statistically-robust magnitude. cladeтАЩs baseline cost sex (mate-finding, diploid reproductive overhead) appears higher parasite selection pressure can offset tested regime. Hamilton (1980) noted sexтАЩs two-fold cost tall order parasites; finding consistent caveat. Pushing тЬЕ require either reducing cladeтАЩs baseline cost sex testing large population sizes mate- finding limiting тАФ deferred 0.6+.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s_asex <- fast_specs()                # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks s_asex$ploidy         <- 1L s_asex$crossover_rate <- 0.0 s_asex$random_seed    <- 42L  s_sex <- fast_specs() s_sex$ploidy         <- 2L s_sex$crossover_rate <- 0.1 s_sex$random_seed    <- 42L  d_asex <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_asex))$ticks d_sex  <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_sex))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_asex[, c("t", "genetic_diversity")], system = "Asexual (haploid)"),   cbind(d_sex[,  c("t", "genetic_diversity")], system = "Sexual (diploid)") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, genetic_diversity, colour = system)) +   geom_line() +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Asexual (haploid)" = "#377eb8",                                  "Sexual (diploid)"  = "#e41a1c"),                       name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Genetic diversity: sexual vs asexual reproduction",        x = "Tick", y = "Genetic diversity") +   theme_minimal()

title: Mimicry and toxicity
text: models. heritable toxicity trait makes prey costly attack. Predators learn signalтАУtoxicity associations via Rescorla-Wagner rule build avoidance. models evolution warning coloration Batesian/ M├╝llerian mimicry (Ruxton et al.┬а2004): toxic prey avoided; non-toxic prey resemble toxic prey also avoided (Batesian mimicry). Key parameters. Expected output (primary claim, post-2026-04-17 reframe). scenario conditionally ЁЯЯа тАФ aposematism evolves clade predation-dominant ecology, grass scarce (grass_rate тЙИ 0.08) predation accounts ~89% mortality rather ~20%. default well-fed ecology, aposematism evolve, Zahavi handicap cost exceeds predation benefit (see тАЬecology-limitedтАЭ Grafen/Sz├бmad├│/Getty theoretical background). vignette now leads predation-dominant regime configuration clade reproduces textbook positive ╬Фtoxicity; default-ecology result documented limit condition. 0.4.4 kernel fixes (vector-signal memory, delta-rule RW, aposematic pleiotropy): P1 (mean toxicity rises predation-dominant ecology): +0.006 600 ticks grass_rate = 0.08, vs тИТ0.001 grass_rate = 0.20 (default well-fed). P3 (avoidance mechanism fires): PASS, 12тАУ28 events per 600 ticks. P4 (positive toxin_dose dose-response): PASS, Spearman ╧Б = +0.40 across dose sweep. See mimicry fidelity report full evidence. primary claim holds one ecology, status remains ЁЯЯа rather тЬЕ тАФ handicap equilibrium fragile theory (Grafen 1990) clade (default ecology), ЁЯЯа honest label. Pre-0.4.4 kernel simplification (historical). 0.4.4, cladeтАЩs predator memory scalar updated toward prey.toxicity directly, removed signal-specific learning тАФ predators couldnтАЩt recognise warning patterns. 0.4.4 refactor replaced full vector-signal memory using Widrow-Hoff delta rule. See тАЬfoundтАЭ details.
code: # Predation-dominant ecology тАФ the regime where aposematism evolves. # At default grass_rate = 0.20, the Zahavi handicap cost exceeds the # protection benefit and toxicity stays flat. Lowering grass to 0.08 # flips the cost-benefit ratio in toxicity's favour. # # Note: this scenario uses default_specs() rather than fast_specs(). # fast_specs() + grass_rate = 0.08 crashes the prey population to # near-extinction тАФ fast_specs shortens prey lifespan, and stacking # predation-dominant ecology on top of that overwhelms viability. # The default_specs timescale (generation ~190 ticks) gives the # population time to persist under heavy predation. s <- default_specs() s$mimicry            <- TRUE s$n_predators_init   <- 5L s$toxicity_init_mean <- 0.1 s$grass_rate         <- 0.08          # predation-dominant ecology s$max_ticks          <- 1000L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)

title: Mimicry and toxicity

title: Module comparison
text: models. Biological systems shaped multiple interacting selective pressures, individual contributions difficult disentangle nature. scenario applies one-module---time factorial design: condition activates exactly one module shared baseline, three summary statistics тАФ mean genetic diversity, mean population size, mean energy тАФ recorded across full run. Comparing modules isolation combining allows moduleтАЩs unique contribution identified; -condition reveals emergent interactions predicted single-module results alone. Key parameters. Expected output. Modules imposing strong directional selection тАФ predators disease тАФ tend increase genetic diversity maintaining selective gradient; modules smooth energy acquisition тАФ niche construction, social learning тАФ may reduce within-population variance. -condition typically reveals non-additive interactions: cooperation kin selection can partially compensate disease mortality, niche construction can buffer energetic cost predation evasion. Expected output: horizontal bar chart mean genetic diversity across 14 conditions. Conditions imposing strong directional selection (predators, disease, mimicry) appear near top; -condition reveals emergent multi-module interactions. found. dashboard shows -condition tick 500 500-tick run (120 agents, 30├Ч30 grid, 5 predators, disease, kin selection, social learning, RL, niche construction, body size evolution active simultaneously): 362 agents survived, 758 total deaths, mean body size evolved 1.0 approximately 1.3. Genetic diversity rose steadily throughout run; lifespan distribution shows majority agents dying age, starvation тАФ indicating population remained energetically healthy despite combined selective load. Across single-module conditions, predators disease produce strongest directional selection (highest mean genetic diversity), social learning niche construction smooth energy gradients reduce within-population variance. -condition simply sum effects: cooperation kin selection partially compensate disease mortality, niche-constructed shelters buffer energetic cost predator evasion, producing population larger diverse predator-disease-conditions.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_specs <- function(...) {   s <- default_specs()   s$max_ticks <- 300L   args <- list(...)   for (nm in names(args)) s[[nm]] <- args[[nm]]   s }  specs_list <- list(   baseline    = make_specs(),   predators   = make_specs(n_predators_init = 5L),   disease     = make_specs(disease = TRUE),   kin         = make_specs(kin_selection = TRUE),   niche       = make_specs(niche_construction = TRUE),   rl          = make_specs(rl_mode = "actor_critic"),   social      = make_specs(social_learning = TRUE),   body_size   = make_specs(body_size_evolution = TRUE),   dispersal   = make_specs(dispersal_evolution = TRUE),   care        = make_specs(parental_care = TRUE),   mimicry     = make_specs(mimicry = TRUE, n_predators_init = 5L),   landscape   = make_specs(complex_landscape = TRUE),   cooperation = make_specs(cooperation_evolution = TRUE),   all_on      = make_specs(     n_predators_init      = 5L,     disease               = TRUE,     kin_selection         = TRUE,     niche_construction    = TRUE,     rl_mode               = "actor_critic",     social_learning       = TRUE,     body_size_evolution   = TRUE,     dispersal_evolution   = TRUE,     cooperation_evolution = TRUE   ) )  results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 4L)  summary_df <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, nm) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     condition      = nm,     mean_diversity = mean(d$genetic_diversity, na.rm = TRUE),     mean_pop       = mean(d$n_agents,          na.rm = TRUE),     mean_energy    = mean(d$mean_energy,       na.rm = TRUE)   ) }, results, names(results), SIMPLIFY = FALSE))  ggplot(summary_df,        aes(x = reorder(condition, mean_diversity), y = mean_diversity)) +   geom_col(fill = "#4dac26") +   coord_flip() +   labs(     title = "Mean genetic diversity by module condition",     x     = NULL,     y     = "Mean genetic diversity"   ) +   theme_minimal()
